worth_it_explanation: In Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform), the Line is the level that carries targeting, budget, bidding, and goal configuration. A Campaign groups Lines and sets shared defaults, including time zone, currency, and budget, that cascade to every Line underneath it. When a Line mixes more than one tactic, channel, or audience type, the platform cannot separate performance by segment, pacing behaves unpredictably, and optimization runs toward a blended signal rather than a clean one. These checks are configuration reviews inside the Yahoo DSP UI that take under an hour and surface problems that compound across the entire flight.
intro: Yahoo DSP organizes buying through a four-level hierarchy: Seat, Advertiser, Campaign, and Line. The Campaign holds default settings, flight dates, and the target key performance indicator (KPI). The Line holds the channel type, targeting parameters, budget, bidding configuration, and the ads that run against those parameters. Each Line can target only one channel, so Display, Video, Connected TV (CTV), Native, and audio buying must each live on a separate Line. These checks confirm that Campaigns and Lines are organized so that optimization, pacing, and reporting align with real tactical boundaries rather than administrative convenience.

audit_checks.title: A single Line mixes targeting parameters that belong to distinct tactics or channels
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Yahoo DSP enforces one channel type per Line, so a Display Line and a CTV Line must be separate by design. The structural problem arises when a practitioner combines multiple audience segments, geographic markets, or bidding strategies on a single Line where they should be separated. Blended Lines make it impossible to read performance by segment, adjust bids by tactic, or apply frequency caps appropriately to each audience or channel.

educate.base:
In Yahoo DSP, a Line is the unit that defines one advertising strategy. When you create a Line, the platform requires you to select a single channel type: Display, Video, Connected TV (CTV), Native, or another supported format. That channel selection is fixed at Line creation and determines which ad formats and targeting options are available inside that Line.

Within a single channel, a Line can carry many targeting parameters simultaneously. You can target by geography, audience segment, device, deal or exchange, frequency cap, and contextual category all on the same Line. The question is not whether a Line can hold multiple parameters, but whether the combination makes optimization and reporting meaningful. A Line targeting both a first-party retargeting audience and a broad open-exchange prospecting audience blends two populations with very different expected conversion rates into a single budget allocation. The platform spends across both, but the performance data does not tell you which population is converting and which is not.

The Microsoft Learn guide for Yahoo DSP buyers, published in collaboration with the Yahoo DSP team, confirms that each Line holds exactly one channel type and that Lines are the level at which targeting, budget, and bid strategy are set. When those parameters mix strategies that should be measured independently, the Line structure is working against the practitioner rather than for them.

A few signs point to a Line that mixes strategies it should not:
  • A single Line targets both a named retargeting audience and broad open-exchange inventory with no exclusion separating them
  • A Line combines geographic markets with different cost-per-action (CPA) benchmarks under a single budget and bid
  • A Line carries audience segments from multiple data providers with different expected match rates and conversion profiles
  • A Line uses a flat cost-per-thousand (CPM) bid where different audience segments would warrant materially different bids
You may want to review each active Line and confirm that it represents one coherent strategy before evaluating its performance data. A Line that cannot be summarized as a single tactic targeting a single audience type may be doing too much.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an e-commerce advertiser, you may want to confirm that prospecting Lines targeting new audiences and retargeting Lines targeting site visitors who did not complete a purchase are separate. Their expected conversion rates differ enough that a shared budget consistently pulls spend away from whichever segment converts at a lower rate. Frequency caps also apply at the Line level, and a blended Line cannot cap frequency separately for cold audiences versus warm ones.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, you may want to confirm that Lines targeting different funnel stages are separated. A Line targeting a broad awareness audience and a Line targeting users who have visited the pricing or contact page serve different purposes and convert at different rates. Combining them under one budget makes the CPA uninterpretable and gives the platform no clean signal on which segment to prioritize.

learn_more.source_type: Microsoft Learn (Xandr, published in collaboration with Yahoo DSP)
learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: campaign and line setup

investigate.intro: Review active Lines and evaluate whether each Line represents a single coherent tactic targeting one primary audience type, or whether it blends strategies that should be separated.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Navigate to an Advertiser and open the Campaigns view. Select a Campaign and open its Lines list.

For each Line, note the channel type (Display, Video, CTV, Native). Confirm that each channel you are running has its own dedicated Line. A single Line cannot run both Display and CTV by platform design, but confirm that no single Line is being used as a catch-all for multiple formats within the same channel.

Open a Line's targeting settings. Review the audience segments attached to it. Note whether the Line targets both a named retargeting audience and broad open-exchange prospecting inventory simultaneously. If so, record it as a blended Line and note the audience types involved.

Check the bid configuration. A Line with a flat CPM bid targeting both a high-intent retargeting audience and cold prospecting inventory is effectively bidding the same amount for two populations with different conversion probabilities. Note this as a structural flag for review.

Review frequency caps on each Line. A Line that mixes audience types cannot apply different frequency caps to each, so a blended Line may over-expose one audience segment while under-exposing another. Note any Line where a single frequency cap is applied to materially different audience types.

evaluate.logic: For each active Line, the practitioner records the channel type, the audience types targeted, and whether the Line combines strategies that should be separated into their own Lines. A Line that targets one audience type or one coherent segment with a consistent bid strategy passes. A Line that blends retargeting and prospecting, combines geographic markets with different CPA benchmarks, or carries audience segments from multiple providers with different conversion profiles is recorded as structurally mixed, with the specific combination noted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each Line's channel type, audience targeting approach, and any mixing of strategies that should be separated to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more active Lines in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account mix targeting strategies that should be separated into distinct Lines. Lines that combine retargeting and prospecting audiences, multiple geographic markets with different cost benchmarks, or audience segments with materially different conversion profiles prevent the platform from optimizing budget allocation accurately and make performance reporting difficult to interpret by segment. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each active Line and separating any that blend strategies into dedicated Lines, one per tactic or audience type, so that bid configuration, frequency caps, and budget allocation align with each segment's distinct behavior.

audit_checks.title: The Campaign does not group Lines so that budget and reporting roll up by tactic or funnel stage
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Campaign-level budget and default settings cascade to every Line underneath. When Lines with different tactics, funnel stages, or audiences share a Campaign without a deliberate organizing principle, campaign-level reporting aggregates performance across segments that should be read separately, and budget caps apply across strategies that benefit from independent controls.

educate.base:
In Yahoo DSP, the Campaign is the container that sets time zone, currency, flight dates, target KPI, and the budget ceiling that all Lines under it share. Lines inherit those Campaign-level defaults unless a Line-level override is applied. The Campaign is also the primary unit that rolls up performance data in reporting: impressions, clicks, and conversions across all Lines in a Campaign aggregate to the Campaign level by default.

A Campaign works best as an organizing unit when every Line inside it shares a single business objective or funnel stage. An awareness Campaign groups Lines built to maximize reach and brand exposure. A conversion Campaign groups Lines built to drive purchase or form-fill events. When a practitioner places awareness and conversion Lines inside the same Campaign, the Campaign-level KPI must be one thing, even though the Lines are built to accomplish different things. Either the KPI misrepresents what the awareness Lines are doing, or it misrepresents what the conversion Lines are doing.

The same logic applies to budget. A Campaign budget cap applies to all Lines simultaneously. If a Campaign holds both a prospecting Line and a retargeting Line, the platform allocates budget across both to hit the Campaign-level budget ceiling. The practitioner cannot easily ring-fence budget for each strategy without building separate Campaigns. Budget that was intended for prospecting may flow to retargeting because that Line generates conversions more quickly, and the Campaign-level budget is consumed before the prospecting Line has had a full flight.

A few signs point to a Campaign that needs to be restructured by tactic or funnel stage:
  • A Campaign name references a brand, quarter, or product rather than a goal, such as 'Q3 Brand' instead of 'Q3 Brand Awareness' or 'Q3 Retargeting'
  • A Campaign holds Lines built for reach alongside Lines built for conversion, with a single KPI applied to both
  • Budget is exhausted unevenly across Lines with no evidence that this reflects the intended allocation
  • Reporting on the Campaign aggregates metrics from prospecting and retargeting Lines, making cost-per-acquisition appear lower than it is for cold audiences
You may want to map each Campaign to a single funnel stage and confirm that all Lines under it share that objective before evaluating campaign-level performance data.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an e-commerce advertiser, a workable structure separates prospecting Campaigns from retargeting Campaigns. Prospecting Campaigns target new audiences and are measured against cost-per-click or cost-per-new-visitor benchmarks. Retargeting Campaigns target users who visited the site and are measured against CPA or return on ad spend (ROAS). Mixing the two under one Campaign produces a blended CPA that understates the true cost of acquiring new customers.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, Campaigns organized by funnel stage give each stage a distinct KPI and budget ring-fence. An awareness Campaign measures reach and video completion. A conversion Campaign measures cost per form submission. Mixing both under one Campaign's budget means neither stage receives what it needs: awareness buys may be underfunded because the budget flows to conversion Lines that show faster results.

learn_more.source_type: Microsoft Learn (Xandr, published in collaboration with Yahoo DSP)
learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: campaign and line setup

investigate.intro: Review each Campaign's organizing principle and confirm that all Lines under it share the same business objective or funnel stage.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Navigate to an Advertiser and open the Campaigns view. For each Campaign, read the name and note whether it implies a single business objective, a funnel stage, or neither. A Campaign named by date, product, or internal code without a goal reference warrants a closer look.

Open each Campaign and review the Lines list. Note the channel types and audience types present. Confirm whether all Lines share a consistent strategy. A Campaign that holds a broad-reach Display Line alongside a CPA-optimized retargeting Line is mixing objectives.

Check the Campaign-level KPI setting. Confirm whether the KPI matches what all Lines in the Campaign are built to accomplish. If any Line is designed to drive a different outcome than the Campaign KPI, record the mismatch.

Review the budget distribution across Lines inside the Campaign. If one Line is consuming a disproportionate share of the budget with no evidence that this is intentional, it may indicate that the Campaign structure is not matching budget allocation to strategic intent.

Confirm flight dates at both the Campaign and Line levels. A Campaign with Lines whose flight dates extend beyond or start before the Campaign's dates indicates that Line-level settings are not aligned with the Campaign's configuration.

evaluate.logic: For each Campaign, the practitioner records the intended business objective or funnel stage and whether all Lines under it share that objective. A Campaign that maps to a single goal with Lines that consistently serve that goal passes. A Campaign that holds Lines built for materially different objectives, that has a KPI mismatched to some of its Lines, or that has no evident organizing principle is recorded as structurally unclear, with the Campaign name, the Lines involved, and the specific mismatch noted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Campaign name, its intended goal or funnel stage, and any Lines whose objective diverges from that goal to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Campaigns in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account group Lines with different objectives or funnel stages under a single budget and KPI. Campaigns that mix awareness and conversion Lines, or prospecting and retargeting Lines, produce reporting that aggregates performance across segments with different success metrics and allow budget to flow unevenly to whichever Line generates faster results rather than to the strategy it was intended to fund. {{voice_recommend}} restructuring the Campaign organization so each Campaign maps to a single funnel stage or business objective, with all Lines inside it sharing that goal, so that the Campaign-level KPI, budget ceiling, and reporting reflect one coherent strategy.

audit_checks.title: The Campaign KPI does not match the goal configuration of the Lines underneath it
audit_checks.impact_rationale: In Yahoo DSP, the Campaign sets the target KPI and that KPI provides the performance benchmark against which Lines are evaluated. When the Campaign KPI references a conversion event that is not receiving activity, or when Lines are configured to optimize toward a goal different from the Campaign KPI, the platform measures success against the wrong signal for the full flight.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP Campaigns are configured with a target KPI that represents the goal the advertiser is buying toward. KPI options span reach and frequency targets, click-based goals, and conversion-based goals tied to events tracked via the Dot pixel or a server-to-server conversion integration. The Campaign KPI is the benchmark against which the platform measures whether the Campaign is on track, and it informs the bid guidance used by Lines operating on a cost-per-action (CPA) model.

A mismatch between the Campaign KPI and the actual behavior of its Lines produces misleading signals. A Campaign set to a reach KPI where the Lines are built to drive CPA performance will show strong delivery against the reach target while providing no signal on whether the conversion goal is being served. A Campaign with a CPA KPI tied to a conversion event that the Dot pixel has not recorded activity against is functionally running without a performance goal. The platform has no conversion data to evaluate the Lines against.

A subtler mismatch appears when a Campaign KPI reflects the right type of goal but references the wrong event. A Campaign whose KPI is set to a product page view event when the advertiser cares about purchases is optimizing toward an action that has no direct relationship to revenue. The Dot pixel may fire on hundreds of page views that never result in a transaction, and the platform treats each as a successful conversion event.

A few signs point to a Campaign KPI that does not match the Line configuration underneath it:
  • The Campaign KPI is set to 'Reach' or a click-based goal while the Lines underneath it are built to drive purchase or form-fill conversions
  • The Campaign KPI references a Dot pixel event that has recorded zero conversions during the current flight
  • The Campaign KPI references a micro-conversion event (e.g., a page view, a video start) when the advertiser's actual business goal is a macro-conversion (e.g., a purchase, a submitted quote request)
  • Lines inside the Campaign have bid strategies set to CPA but the Dot pixel event attached to the conversion goal has not fired recently
You may want to confirm that the Dot pixel event attached to the Campaign KPI is receiving activity before evaluating whether the Lines are performing. A KPI tied to an inactive conversion event gives the impression that the Campaign is not converting when the real problem may be that the tracking event is not firing.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an e-commerce advertiser, the Campaign KPI should reference a purchase or revenue-based Dot pixel event, not a product page view or an add-to-cart event, unless the Campaign is explicitly designed as an upper-funnel awareness buy. You may want to confirm that the conversion event attached to the KPI corresponds to a completed transaction and that it is recording activity during the current flight.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the Campaign KPI should reference the form submission or qualified lead Dot pixel event, not a thank-you page visit or a click event. You may want to confirm that the conversion event captures the actual submission rather than a downstream page that users may reach through paths that do not include a completed form.

learn_more.source_type: Microsoft Learn (Xandr, published in collaboration with Yahoo DSP)
learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: campaign KPI and line setup

investigate.intro: Confirm that the Campaign KPI matches the advertiser's actual business objective, that the Dot pixel event attached to the KPI is recording activity, and that the Lines inside the Campaign are configured to optimize toward that same goal.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Open a Campaign in Yahoo DSP and review the KPI setting. Note the goal type (reach, click-based, or CPA) and, for CPA goals, the specific Dot pixel event it references.

Navigate to the Advertiser's Dot pixel settings and confirm that the conversion event attached to the Campaign KPI has recorded activity during the current flight. A CPA goal with zero conversion events means the platform has no signal to evaluate against. Note the event name and its conversion count.

Review the bid configuration for each Line inside the Campaign. Confirm that Lines using a CPA bid strategy reference the same conversion event as the Campaign KPI. A discrepancy between the Campaign KPI event and the Line-level bid goal event is a flag for goal misalignment.

Check whether the conversion event referenced by the Campaign KPI is the business outcome the advertiser actually cares about. A KPI tied to a page view or a video start when the advertiser's goal is purchases means the Campaign is measuring the wrong thing. Note the event name and the intended business outcome.

If the Campaign KPI is a reach or frequency goal, confirm that the Lines inside the Campaign are built to serve awareness or reach objectives rather than direct-response conversion goals. A reach-KPI Campaign holding CPA-bid Lines is a signal that the KPI was not updated when the Line configuration changed.

evaluate.logic: For each Campaign, the practitioner records the KPI type, the Dot pixel event it references (for CPA goals), the event's conversion count during the current flight, and whether the Lines inside the Campaign are configured to optimize toward the same goal. A Campaign with a KPI that matches the advertiser's business objective, a conversion event recording activity, and Lines whose bid strategy aligns with the Campaign goal passes. A Campaign whose KPI mismatches the advertiser's objective, whose conversion event has zero activity, or whose Lines use a bid strategy inconsistent with the Campaign KPI is recorded as failing, with the specific mismatch noted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Campaign KPI type, the Dot pixel event it references, the event's conversion count for the current flight, and any Lines whose bid strategy diverges from the Campaign goal to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Campaigns in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account have a KPI setting that does not match the advertiser's actual business objective, or have a Dot pixel conversion event attached to the KPI that has not recorded activity during the current flight. A Campaign KPI tied to an inactive or mismatched conversion event means the platform has no reliable signal against which to evaluate Line performance, and bid strategies set to cost-per-action on those Lines are effectively running without a goal. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the KPI setting on each active Campaign, confirming that the Dot pixel event attached to the KPI reflects the primary business outcome and is recording activity, and separating any Campaigns whose Lines are built to optimize toward materially different goals into distinct Campaigns with KPIs calibrated to each objective.
